**[I. Purpose and Scope]{.underline}**

This memorandum summarizes the rationale behind the dispute resolution provisions drafted by Harmon & Grey LLP across the three principal transaction documents comprising the Greenleaf Nutritionals / Pacifica Botanics strategic supply relationship: (i) the Exclusive Supply Agreement (the \"**SA**\"), (ii) the Quality Assurance Side Letter (the \"**QA Letter**\"), and (iii) the IP License Agreement (the \"**IPLA**\"), each to be entered into between Pacifica Botanics Pte. Ltd. (\"**Pacifica**\") and Greenleaf Nutritionals, Inc. (\"**Greenleaf**\").

Harmon & Grey drafted the initial arbitration and dispute resolution clauses across all three documents at Pacifica\'s instruction. This memorandum is being shared with Greenleaf\'s counsel, Whitfield & Crane LLP, to facilitate their review and to expedite negotiation of any proposed revisions in advance of the anticipated signing date of **July 18, 2025**, and the Target Effective Date of **August 1, 2025**. We respectfully request that Whitfield & Crane provide any comments on the dispute resolution provisions no later than July 11, 2025, to allow sufficient time for resolution before signing.

By way of commercial context, the transaction establishes a seven-year exclusive supply relationship with minimum annual purchase commitments of USD \$22,000,000 during Years 1 through 3 and USD \$28,000,000 during Years 4 through 7, for aggregate minimum commitments of approximately USD \$178,000,000 over the initial term. The SA further provides for two automatic three-year renewal periods, subject to certain conditions. Given the scale and duration of this relationship, the dispute resolution architecture was crafted with particular care to ensure that any disagreements between the parties are resolved efficiently, predictably, and with access to decision-makers possessing the appropriate institutional and subject-matter expertise.

**[II. Overview of the Dispute Resolution Framework]{.underline}**

The transaction documents employ a three-tier dispute resolution structure, with each agreement directing disputes to the institution and forum best suited to its subject matter. This architecture was designed deliberately to match the nature of the obligations under each agreement with the institutional competence and procedural tools most likely to produce efficient and well-reasoned outcomes.

**A. Supply Agreement --- Article 14 (ICC Arbitration)**

The SA, which constitutes the core commercial framework governing pricing, delivery schedules, exclusivity obligations, and termination rights, provides for arbitration under the Rules of Arbitration of the International Chamber of Commerce (\"**ICC**\"). The ICC\'s established reputation for administering complex international commercial disputes, its experienced Secretariat, and its global reach make it the natural institution for the principal commercial agreement in this transaction.

Pursuant to SA Section 14.1, any dispute arising out of or relating to the SA shall be finally resolved by arbitration administered by the ICC. The juridical seat of arbitration is Singapore (Section 14.2), selected because Pacifica is incorporated in Singapore and the curial law of Singapore provides a well-developed, pro-arbitration framework for international arbitration. The venue of hearings shall be London, England, given its position as a leading centre for international arbitration and its geographic midpoint between the parties\' principal operations. London was chosen as a neutral, convenient hearing location offering world-class arbitration facilities and infrastructure, and the distinction between the seat and the hearing location is a routine feature of modern international arbitration practice.

Section 14.3 provides that the arbitration shall be conducted by a sole arbitrator, with either party retaining the right to request a panel of three arbitrators within fifteen (15) days of service of the Request for Arbitration. This approach offers the parties flexibility, allowing straightforward disputes to proceed efficiently before a sole arbitrator while preserving the option for a full panel in matters of greater complexity or value.

The governing law of the SA is the law of the State of New York, United States, without regard to conflict-of-laws principles (Section 14.4). This reflects the fact that Greenleaf\'s commercial operations are centered in the United States and New York law is widely understood and applied in international commercial disputes of this nature.

The QA Letter, which governs product specifications, testing methodologies, acceptance criteria, and rejection rights, employs a specialized two-step dispute resolution mechanism under the auspices of the Singapore International Arbitration Centre (\"**SIAC**\").

"""Generate a .docx from markdown via Pandoc with an optional reference template.

Usage:
    python generate_from_md.py input.md output.docx [template.docx]

Pandoc applies the template's paragraph and run styles to your markdown.
Best for reports/memos/letters where the template carries firm styling.

Requires pandoc on PATH.
"""
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def generate(md_path: Path, output_path: Path, template_path: Path | None = None):
    output_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    cmd = ["pandoc", str(md_path), "-o", str(output_path)]
    if template_path is not None:
        cmd.append(f"--reference-doc={template_path}")
    result = subprocess.run(cmd, capture_output=True, text=True)
    if result.returncode != 0:
        print(f"pandoc failed: {result.stderr}", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(1)
    print(f"OK: wrote {output_path}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    if len(sys.argv) not in (3, 4):
        print("Usage: generate_from_md.py <input.md> <output.docx> [template.docx]", file=sys.stderr)
        sys.exit(2)
    template = Path(sys.argv[3]) if len(sys.argv) == 4 else None
    generate(Path(sys.argv[1]), Path(sys.argv[2]), template)

### M8. The \$5M escalation threshold is arbitrary and undercut by the open-ended challenge trigger

**Severity: Medium**

**Provision(s):** QA Side Letter § 7.2; Greenleaf Emails (Park recall example).

**Issue:** QA Side Letter § 7.2 escalates to arbitration if a dispute "involves an amount in excess of Five Million United States Dollars (\$5,000,000), or if the expert determination... is challenged by either Party."

**Analysis:** The \$5M threshold is a fixed figure that does not adjust over the 13-year term and does not match the realistic value of recall disputes --- Ridgeway's portfolio example involved over \$14M in consequential losses alone. More importantly, the alternative "challenge by either Party" trigger makes the monetary threshold largely moot: any party can escalate regardless of amount. The threshold therefore neither reliably channels small disputes to expert determination nor reliably routes large disputes to arbitration.

**Recommendation:** If expert determination is to be a meaningful filter (per M7), tie escalation to objective criteria (e.g., the \$5M threshold, indexed over time, or a finding of procedural irregularity) rather than an open-ended challenge right. If the parties prefer arbitration for all material disputes, simplify to a single arbitration track and use expert determination only as a non-binding advisory input.

### M9. No institutional emergency-arbitrator provisions invoked

**Severity: Medium**

**Provision(s):** SA Art. 14; QA Side Letter § 7; IPLA § 9.

**Issue:** None of the clauses invokes the emergency-arbitrator mechanisms available under the ICC, SIAC, or LCIA Rules.

**Analysis:** Each of the three institutions offers an emergency-arbitrator procedure for urgent relief before the tribunal is constituted. For supply disruptions, IP misuse, or urgent quarantine/recall situations, an emergency arbitrator can provide faster relief than a court in some cases and keeps the dispute within the arbitral framework. SA § 14.7 preserves court interim relief but does not provide the institutional alternative.

**Recommendation:** Expressly incorporate the relevant institution's emergency-arbitrator rules in each arbitration clause, alongside the court interim-relief carve-out (see H5).

### M10. Ridgeway Capital Partners (62% equity holder / Affiliate) has no intervention mechanism

**Severity: Medium**

**Provision(s):** Greenleaf Emails (Park); IPLA § 1 (Affiliate definition); SA § 1.1 (Affiliate).

**Issue:** Ridgeway Capital Partners holds approximately 62% of Greenleaf and is an "Affiliate" of Greenleaf under both agreements, but it is not a party to any of the three agreements. Park notes that if Greenleaf's and Ridgeway's interests diverge on a settlement question, "there's no procedural mechanism to address that."

**Analysis:** Park himself characterizes this as "not a primary concern," and arbitration is generally a party-only process. However, the absence of any intervention/notice mechanism means a controlling equity holder with a direct economic stake has no procedural voice. This is a minor governance gap rather than a dispute-resolution defect per se.

**Recommendation:** Consider a notice-to-sponsor obligation (requiring Greenleaf to notify Ridgeway of material disputes) and, if desired, a limited non-party observation right. Do not create an intervention right that would complicate the arbitration. This is the lowest priority of the Medium items.

## VII. Low Issues

### L1. QA Side Letter expert-independence standard may be impractical for a niche field

**Severity: Low**

**Provision(s):** QA Side Letter § 7.1.

**Issue:** § 7.1 requires the Expert to "not have any current or prior relationship with either Party or their respective affiliates, counsel, or testing laboratories."

**Analysis:** Botanical extract manufacturing, analytical chemistry, and dietary-supplement quality assurance is a relatively small field. A "no prior relationship... with... testing laboratories" standard, applied strictly, could make it difficult to appoint a genuinely qualified expert who has never interacted with either party's testing labs. An overly strict standard risks appointment deadlock and SIAC appointment delays.

**Recommendation:** Soften to a requirement that the Expert be independent and impartial, disclose any past or present relationship with the Parties or their affiliates/counsel/labs, and be disqualifiable only for actual conflict --- rather than an absolute "no prior relationship" bar.

### L2. IPLA specifies the seat (London) but not the hearing venue

**Severity: Low**

**Provision(s):** IPLA § 9.1.

**Issue:** IPLA § 9.1 fixes the seat as London but does not specify a hearing venue (unlike SA § 14.2, which fixes London as the venue).

**Analysis:** Minor. The LCIA Rules default the venue to the seat (London) absent party agreement, so this is not a material gap, but specifying it would improve consistency with the SA's drafting style.

**Recommendation:** Specify the hearing venue in the IPLA (London, consistent with the seat) for consistency, or leave to LCIA default if harmonizing to a single seat under C1.

### L3. Indemnification procedures (SA § 10.3) control third-party claim defense in court while direct disputes go to arbitration

**Severity: Low**

**Provision(s):** SA § 10.3; SA § 14.8.

**Issue:** SA § 10.3 governs the defense of third-party claims under the indemnification provisions (court-based defense control, settlement-consent rights). Direct Party-to-Party disputes go to arbitration under Article 14.

**Analysis:** There is a potential court/arbitration split: third-party claim defense is managed under § 10.3 (court), while the indemnification obligation itself (if disputed) is arbitrated under Article 14. The § 14.8 loser-pays fee-shifting may also interact with the § 10.3 "reasonable attorneys' fees" indemnity to create potential double-recovery or allocation questions. This is a minor structural point.

**Analysis:** These clauses are circular. The QA Side Letter says its
own dispute-resolution mechanism (SIAC) controls for quality disputes;
the SA says the SA (ICC) controls for all conflicts; the IPLA says the
IPLA controls for matters it "exclusively" addresses (which would
include its own LCIA clause). The result is that, for any dispute
touching more than one document, each party can point to a hierarchy
clause favoring its preferred forum. This is precisely the kind of
ambiguity that produces jurisdictional/competence challenges at the
outset of an arbitration --- the "pathological clause" problem that, per
the HG Memo and Greenleaf Emails, caused Pacifica a 14-month enforcement
delay in its 2023 Hong Kong-seated ICC arbitration against a Korean
buyer.

**Recommendation:** Replace all three hierarchy clauses with a single,
unified conflict-priority provision, identically worded in each
document, that (a) establishes a clear order of precedence and (b)
expressly addresses which dispute-resolution regime governs
multi-document disputes (see C4, H4). The unified clause should be
drafted to eliminate any basis for a competence challenge.

### C4. Direct conflict between the IPLA consolidation clause and the SA arbitration clause --- pathological-clause risk

**Severity: Critical**

**Provision(s):** IPLA § 9.3; SA § 14.1; SA § 15.8; IPLA § 10.3.

**Issue:** IPLA § 9.3 provides that "claims arising under this Agreement
that also relate to obligations under the Supply Agreement shall be
heard jointly in a single arbitration proceeding under this Section 9"
